claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child is 16 and in approved education
Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to claim for, please tell me each of their ages (you can also share their names so I can keep track).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my two children - Alex, who's 6 years old, and Blake, who's 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're very welcome – I'm happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me work through each child's situation:

---

**For Alex (6 years old):**

Since Alex is under 16, they automatically meet the age requirement. Let me check the next step for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Alex (aged 6), I need to ask:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given Alex is 6 years old, I'd expect the answer is yes – but please confirm!

---

**For Blake (16 years old):**

Since Blake is between 16 and 19, I'll need to check their education or training status. Let me get that information ready.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Blake (16 years old), I need to know:**

Is Blake currently:
- **In education or training** (such as school, college, or an approved training programme)?
- **Recently left education** (within the last few months)?
- **Neither** – not in education/training and hasn't been recently?

---

Please let me know:
1. **Alex** – Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not working at all)?
2. **Blake** – Is Blake in education/training, recently left education, or neither?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, Alex is only 6, so no, he's not working at all - he's at primary school!

As for Blake, yes, she's still in education. She's doing her A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for clarifying!

**For Alex (6 years old):**

Since Alex isn't working at all, he meets this requirement. Let me continue checking the next criteria for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now for Alex, the next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)

For a 6-year-old, this would typically be "no, he's not receiving those benefits" – please confirm!

---

**For Blake (16 years old, doing A-levels at sixth form college):**

Let me check the education level requirements for Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent news for Blake! **A-levels are on the approved qualifications list** for Child Benefit eligibility.

For Blake, I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. **Is Blake studying full-time?** (This means 12 or more hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is the course NOT funded by an employer** (and Blake doesn't have an agreement to work for an employer after finishing)?

Typically, A-levels at a sixth form college would meet these requirements, but please confirm!

---

**So to summarise, please confirm:**
1. **Alex** – Is he NOT receiving benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own right? (Almost certainly not at age 6!)
2. **Blake** – Is she studying full-time (12+ hours/week) and the course isn't employer-funded?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, that's right - Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's only 6!

And yes, Blake is studying full-time at sixth form college - it's definitely more than 12 hours a week of classes and supervised study. No, it's not funded by an employer - it's just normal sixth form.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming all of that!

Let me continue working through the requirements for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good, moving along nicely!

**For Alex:** He's not receiving benefits, so he passes this check. The next question is about apprenticeships – but since Alex is 6 and at primary school, he's clearly not in an apprenticeship!

**For Blake:** She's doing A-levels (approved education), studying full-time, and it's not employer-funded – great! And since she's at sixth form college doing A-levels, she's also not in an apprenticeship.

Let me continue to the next check for both children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now we need to check **your** (the claimant's) situation.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You'll qualify if **any one** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively looking for work (can claim for up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient financial resources
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

The most common situation is simply **normally living in the UK**. Does that apply to you, or does one of the other situations fit better?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK. I'm living abroad at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. Living abroad can still allow you to claim Child Benefit in some circumstances.

**Are you a Crown servant living abroad?**

This would include roles such as:
- A diplomat or other government official posted overseas
- A member of the UK armed forces stationed abroad
- Other government employees working in certain qualifying countries

Does this apply to your situation, or are you living abroad for a different reason?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or anything like that. I'm just living abroad for personal reasons.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, based on what you've told me, this is an important factor in determining your eligibility.

To be eligible for UK Child Benefit, you need to meet the UK residency requirements. The qualifying criteria are:

1. ❌ **Normally living in the UK** – You've confirmed you don't live in the UK
2. ❌ **Settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. ❌ **Pre-settled status** (with various conditions like working, seeking work, studying, etc.)
4. ❌ **Crown servant living abroad** – You've confirmed this doesn't apply

Since you're living abroad for personal reasons and aren't a Crown servant, I'm afraid you don't meet the residency requirements for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Assessment Outcome: **Not Eligible**

I'm sorry to share this news, but based on our assessment, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit** for either Alex or Blake.

### Summary

| Child | Child Qualifies? | You Can Claim? |
|-------|------------------|----------------|
| **Alex** (age 6) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Blake** (age 16) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |

### Why?

Both of your children meet the eligibility criteria:
- **Alex** qualifies as he's under 16
- **Blake** qualifies as she's in approved full-time education (A-levels)

However, **you** don't meet the UK residency requirements because:
- You don't normally live in the UK
- You're not a Crown servant posted abroad
- You don't have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme

### What Can You Do?

1. **If your circumstances change** – If you return to live in the UK, you would likely become eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children (assuming their circumstances remain the same).

2. **If someone else cares for the children in the UK** – If Alex and Blake live with someone else in the UK who is responsible for them (such as a partner, relative, or guardian), that person may be able to claim Child Benefit instead.

3. **Check for other support** – Depending on where you're living, you may be entitled to family benefits in that country.

For more information, you can visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/eligibility

I'm sorry I couldn't bring better news. Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify or help with?

reasonings: Alex (age 6) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: he is under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (age 16) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: she is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college), studying full-time (12+ hours per week), the course is not employer-funded, she is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, and she is not receiving benefits in her own right. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Both children (Alex and Blake) meet the eligibility criteria from their perspective – Alex as a child under 16, and Blake as a 16-year-old in approved full-time education. However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements, which is a mandatory criterion for receiving Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons, is not a Crown servant, and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. As a result, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.reason: Does not meet UK residency requirements
response.guidance: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances (settled status, pre-settled status with qualifying criteria, Crown servant, or living in certain countries). If you have pre-settled status, you may need to be working, studying, or have sufficient resources.

However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (age 16) meets all the child-related eligibility criteria: she is in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college), studying full-time (12+ hours per week), the course is not employer-funded, she is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, and she is not receiving benefits in her own right. However, the claimant (parent) does not meet the UK residency requirements. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons and is not a Crown servant, nor do they have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible to receive Child Benefit for Blake.

However, the claimant does not meet the UK residency requirements, which is a mandatory criterion for receiving Child Benefit. The claimant is living abroad for personal reasons, is not a Crown servant, and does not have settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme. As a result, the claimant is not eligible to claim Child Benefit for either child.
